text: A natural hollow in the rock face. The ground inside is dry.
The fallen oak makes a roof. Dry leaves banked on the lee side.
The cliff face overhangs here. Dry ground beneath — maybe two arm-lengths deep.
Dense yew, dark and close. The wind can't reach inside. The ground is dry.
The overhang faces south. Wind from the north won't touch it. Room enough to lie down, and the rock will hold the day's warmth.
Bracken enough for a good bed. The hollow blocks three sides of the wind. This would hold in rain.
Good camp here. The slope drains away from it. The stream is close but not too close.
Crab apples in the hedgerow. Small, sour.
Dock leaves thick at the stream edge. The roots are starchy.
Hazelnuts. Not ripe yet — but close.
Blackberries on the south face of the scrub. The ones in full sun are ripe.
Sorrel growing thick in the grass. Sharp flavour. Good raw.
Wild garlic — the smell carries before you see it. Under the trees, along the stream.
Crab apples past their best, but a frost will have sweetened them. Worth remembering this spot.
Meadowsweet by the water. Good for fever — the leaves dried.
Tracks in the soft ground. Cloven hooves. Something heavy.
A bird of prey circling. Something moving in the grass below.
The undergrowth moved. Too large for a rabbit.
Droppings on the path. Deer, or something similar.
Deer tracks heading uphill — a doe, probably. The stride is narrow. The prints are fresh.
Birdsong stopped thirty paces back. Something is watching from the treeline.
An otter slide on the bank. Fish bones at the water's edge. The water is clean here.
The deer path leads downhill. It will go to water. Worn enough to follow.
Something in the air here. Hard to name.
The backs of your knees feel strange. The ground is level — but something is off.
A prickling on the back of your neck. The air is still.
Your skin prickles. Not cold. This place is not empty.
The feeling from the cave. Here again, fainter — but the same.
The ground feels thin here. You know this feeling now.
The ground is worn here. Not a path — but something passes this way.
An animal trail through the bracken. Going downhill.
A line in the turf. Old, but the direction is there.
An old trackway. The turf is different where feet have compressed the soil for years.
The ridge continues northeast. Firm ground, good walking.
This valley connects to the one you came through. The watershed will be to the east.
The wind has shifted. West now, where it was from the north.
Cloud building to the southwest. It'll be here by evening.
The air feels different. Closer.
Two days of this at least. The cloud base is low and getting lower.
Rain before dark. The air smells of wet earth from the west.
Good walking weather for now. The wind will hold it off until evening.
Nettles at the stream edge. Edible if cooked — worth knowing.
Hazel catkins. The nuts will come in late summer.
Wild thyme on the chalk. Useful — good for cough, and it dries well.
Woundwort near the stream. The wise woman uses this for wounds. Worth gathering.
These roots are best dug after frost — the cold breaks the starch down. Remember this spot.
Wood sorrel in the shade. Sharp and fresh. Good eating raw.
